task: I’m dealing with a subcontractor who wants to keep working after I’ve already flagged a hazard, and I want to handle it cleanly without making the situation worse. Can you help me outline what I should put in writing and what the next step usually is if they keep pushing back?

turns.agent_response:
Yes. Keep it factual and tied to control.

## What to put in writing
- date, time, and location
- the hazard you identified
- who was notified
- what was required to stop or correct it
- whether work was paused
- what condition must be met before restart
- who is responsible for the response
- deadline for response or correction

## What the notice should say
- the hazard is not approved for continued work
- work may not continue until the condition is controlled and verified
- verbal assurance is not enough
- any restart requires confirmation from the responsible person

## If they keep pushing back
### First pushback
- restate the hazard and the required control
- repeat that work stays paused until verified

### Second pushback or refusal
- escalate to subcontractor leadership
- notify your supervisor, PM, or safety lead
- document the refusal or disagreement

### If the hazard is life-safety or they ignore stop-work
- stop the work immediately
- escalate the same day
- do not negotiate around the hazard

## Simple line to use
- **“The hazard is flagged. Work does not continue until it is controlled and verified.”**
- **“I am not accepting verbal assurance as clearance.”**
- **“If you want to continue, show the control.”**

## Keep the record
Save:
- the notice
- any reply
- photos or inspection notes
- who was told
- when the condition was verified or escalated

If you want, I can turn this into a **short notice template** you can send by email or text.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I want the actual wording tightened up so it holds up if this turns into a dispute.

Give me a short notice template I can send by email/text, with the hard stop language and a clean escalation line if they argue. Keep it blunt, not chatty. Also include what I should ask them to confirm back in writing before anything restarts.

turns.agent_response:
Sure. Here’s a blunt version you can send:

---

**Subject:** Work Stop / Hazard Control Required

A hazard has been identified at **[location]** on **[date/time]**. Work in this area is **stopped** until the condition is controlled and verified.

Do not continue work on this item. Verbal assurance is not clearance. Before any restart, send written confirmation of:
- the control in place
- who installed or verified it
- when it was completed
- that the area is safe for normal work

If you disagree with this stop, escalate it through your supervisor and project management. The work remains stopped until the hazard is corrected and confirmed.

**[Your name]**  
**[Title]**

---

If you want, I can also make this:
1. more formal for email, or  
2. even shorter for text message.

user_simulator.text:
Close, but I want it a little tighter and more defensible. That line "if you disagree" is fine, but I want the escalation to read like a hard procedural step, not a discussion invite.

Give me a version with:
- a subject line for email
- a 2-3 sentence text version
- one escalation line that makes it clear they can push it up the chain, but the stop stays in place
- a specific written confirmation request before restart, like who verified the control and that the hazard is cleared for work

Keep it blunt. No fluff.

turns.agent_response:
Yes. Use this:

### Email subject
**Work Stopped – Hazard Control Required**

### Email / notice
A hazard has been identified at **[location]** on **[date/time]**. Work in this area is **stopped** until the condition is controlled and verified. Verbal assurance is not clearance.

Before restart, send written confirmation of:
- the control in place
- who verified it
- when it was verified
- that the hazard is cleared and the area is safe for work

If you want to challenge the stop, escalate it through your chain. **The stop remains in place until the hazard is corrected and confirmed.**

### Text version
Work in **[location]** is stopped بسبب a hazard identified on **[date/time]**. Do not continue until it is controlled and verified. Send written confirmation of the control, who verified it, when verified, and that the area is cleared for work. If you want to challenge the stop, escalate through your chain; the stop stays in place.

### Escalation line
**Any challenge to this stop goes through the chain of command. The stop remains in effect until the hazard is corrected and verified.**

If you want, I can make this even shorter for a text-only send.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the right shape. Tight, defensible, no invitation to argue.

I can use that as-is.
